description: You are preparing an eclipse observation guide for the August 12, 2026 Arctic Total Solar Eclipse. Read the eclipse data from /home/ga/data/historical_eclipses.json to find the event timing and best viewing location. Set Stellarium to the best viewing location listed in the data file. Configure the display: atmosphere ON, landscape/ground OFF, constellation lines ON. Starting approximately one hour before the greatest eclipse time (from the data file), systematically advance time to determine all four eclipse contact points by visually inspecting the Sun: C1 (partial eclipse begins, Moon's limb first touches the Sun), C2 (totality begins, Sun fully obscured by the Moon), C3 (totality ends, first sliver of sunlight reappears), C4 (partial eclipse ends, Moon completely clears the Sun). At each contact, record the UTC time and the Sun's altitude and azimuth. During totality (C2-C3), zoom out and identify all bright planets or stars visible in the darkened sky near the eclipsed Sun. Take at least one screenshot at each contact plus one wide-field shot during totality (minimum 5 screenshots). As a control, change location to Rome, Italy (41.90 N, 12.50 E) at the mid-eclipse time and document the eclipse appearance from this location. Take a screenshot. Write a complete analysis to ~/Desktop/eclipse_contact_times.txt including: all four contact UTC times with Sun altitude/azimuth, totality duration (C3 minus C2), objects visible during totality, and the Rome control observation result.

metadata.data_source: NASA Eclipse Database (eclipse.gsfc.nasa.gov) - Five Millennium Canon of Solar Eclipses
